title: Working Papers
text:
Drafts available on request.

Scarcity, Worthiness, and the Politics of Refugee Admission in the Global North
Andrew S. Rosenberg
R&R, Journal of Politics
Examines why some refugee crises prompt generosity while others trigger restriction. Develops a formal model incorporating scarcity costs, refugee racial identity, and geopolitical salience, and tests predictions using country-year asylum policy data.

Email for draft



The Discretionary Color Line in International Institutions
Andrew S. Rosenberg
Tests whether formally equal rules eliminate racial hierarchy in practice, using the Schengen visa regime as a case study. Ancestral distance predicts visa refusal rates, with busier consulates discriminating more.

Email for draft



Traces of the Past: Regime Histories and Anti-Foreigner Violence in Post-Unification Germany
Hannah M. Alarian, Michael Bernhard, and Andrew S. Rosenberg
Investigates whether distinct regime histories explain disparate patterns of anti-foreigner violence in post-unification Germany, examining the impact of East–West “coming to terms with the past” approaches.


Who Deserves to Belong? Public Opinion and U.S. Naturalization Decisions
Juliana K. Mucci, Hannah M. Alarian, and Andrew S. Rosenberg


National Pride and Prejudice: How National Identity Moderates Anti-Immigrant Attitudes
Andrew S. Rosenberg and Hannah M. Alarian
Survey experiments testing whether connecting immigration to the NHS reduces anti-immigrant sentiment among strong national identifiers.


Immigration Does Not Lower Social Capital
Andrew S. Rosenberg
Uses county-level data and an instrumental variable design to challenge the assumption that immigration diminishes social capital.


Races at the Bottom: Local Impacts of Official Discrimination on Election Outcomes
Conor O’Dwyer, Andrew S. Rosenberg, and Matthew Stenberg
Analyzes Polish “LGBT Free Zones” and their impact on Law and Justice party election outcomes in 2024 municipal elections.


Voting for Extremists: How Extremist Protests Influence Far-Right Voting Behavior in Germany
Hannah M. Alarian, Sebastian Elischer, and Andrew S. Rosenberg
Examines AfD proximity to right-wing extremist organizations and its electoral impact across election cycles 2013–2021.

title: Software
text:
An R package implementing methods from Rosenberg, Knuppe, and Braumoeller (2017), Unifying the Study of Asymmetric Hypotheses, Political Analysis. The package provides routines for specifying, estimating, and testing asymmetric relationships in social-science data.

title: Andrew S. Rosenberg
text: Associate Professor of Political Science · University of Florida

title: Andrew S. Rosenberg
text:
About
I am an Associate Professor and Director of Graduate Studies in the Department of Political Science at the University of Florida. My research examines the intersection of race and racism in international politics, global inequality, migration policy, forced displacement, and international order. My work has appeared in the American Journal of Political Science, Political Analysis, International Studies Quarterly, Journal of Peace Research, and other journals.
My first book, Undesirable Immigrants: Why Racism Persists in International Migration (Princeton University Press, 2022), received the 2023 Best Book Award from the Race, Ethnicity, and Politics section of the American Political Science Association. I am writing a second book, Hierarchies of Worth: Race and the Politics of Costly State Action.
I received my Ph.D. from Ohio State University, an M.Sc. (Research) in International Relations from the London School of Economics, and a B.A. in International Studies and Political Science from Johns Hopkins University. I grew up in Des Moines, Iowa.

title: Andrew S. Rosenberg
text:
Teaching
I teach graduate methods and IR theory at Florida. Recent and current courses include Topics in Political Methodology (POS 6747) (Spring 2026), Linear Models, Political Network Analysis, and a pre-term Math Workshop. Past undergraduate offerings include Networks in International Relations and Data Visualization and Literacy. Syllabi are available on request — see Teaching →.

title: Books
text:
Undesirable Immigrants: Why Racism Persists in International Migration. Princeton University Press, 2022. International History and Politics series.
Princeton University Press · Amazon

Best Book Award, 2023, Race, Ethnicity, and Politics section, American Political Science Association.

A book about why international migration remains stratified by race long after the formal end of racially explicit immigration laws. Drawing on quantitative analysis of bilateral migration, visa, and asylum policies — together with historical case material — Undesirable Immigrants argues that racial hierarchy has been reproduced through ostensibly neutral administrative practice.
Profiled in Mother Jones and discussed on the New Books in Political Science podcast.

text:

Drawing on quantitative analysis of bilateral migration, visa, and asylum policies — together with historical case material — Undesirable Immigrants argues that racial hierarchy has been reproduced through ostensibly neutral administrative practice.

title: Books
text:
Hierarchies of Worth
Hierarchies of Worth: Race and the Politics of Costly State Action. In progress.
Develops a theory of why some crises generate extraordinary state action while others produce neglect, exclusion, or procedural restraint. Examines the interaction between economic scarcity, foreign-policy incentives, and racialized moral worth judgments, extending debates on race and the liberal international order.

Develops a measurement tool demonstrating that migrants from majority-Black states migrate less than expected.

title: Teaching
text: My courses have been awarded an Affordable UF badge for materials costing less than $20 per credit hour.

title: Teaching
text:
Graduate

Topics in Political Methodology (POS 6747) — Spring 2026. Graduate-level introduction to statistical modeling, with a focus on OLS regression. Built around Gelman, Hill, and Vehtari’s Regression and Other Stories.
Political Data Analysis — graduate methods sequence, applied statistics and reproducible workflows in R.
Linear Models — graduate course in the linear regression framework and its extensions.
International Relations Theory — graduate proseminar in IR theory.
Political Network Analysis — graduate seminar on network methods for political research.
Math Workshop — pre-term mathematics review for incoming graduate students.

title: Teaching
text:
Undergraduate

Networks in International Relations — IR through the lens of network analysis.
Data Visualization and Literacy — making and reading quantitative evidence in the social sciences.

Syllabi and reading lists are available on request.
